title: What New York City taught me about Dutch values
gist: Using an anecdote about a Dutch center-left minister who insisted there is no 'Dutch Way' for immigrants to assimilate to, Yglesias argues that national and sub-national identities like New York's Dutch-rooted ethos of tolerance are simultaneously very real and totally invented - the 'imagined communities' point that history is both factual and a matter of narrative choice.

insights.claim: A junior Dutch PvdA minister argued immigration is harder for European social democrats than for Americans because the US has an explicit propositional nationalism ('truth, justice, and the American Way') while there is no equivalent 'Dutch Way' for immigrants to assimilate toward.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Highlights why assimilation politics differ structurally between propositional-nationalism states and ethnically-defined ones - a framing for European integration and far-right risk.
insights.quote: America has a streak of explicit propositional nationalism to it
insights.locator: At one point the group was meeting

insights.claim: Yglesias counters that there is a usable 'Dutch Way' because New York frames its traditions of tolerance and diversity as inherited Dutch values, suggesting immigrants can be told they are welcome but must practice open-mindedness 'for Dutch reasons.'
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Argues civic identities can be constructed to support assimilation even in ethnically-defined nations - an angle on how Europe could frame integration.
insights.quote: they are welcome in the city for Dutch reasons but also must learn to practice open-mindedness and tolerance for Dutch reasons

insights.claim: National and sub-national identities are simultaneously very real and totally made up, and the idea of history is linked to storytelling - good history should be accurate but also involves narrative choices beyond questions of fact.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A reminder that durable collective identities are constructed narratives - useful for reading how nationalism and civic myth-making get deployed politically.
insights.quote: these national or sub-national identities are simultaneously very real and also totally made up
insights.locator: This was an interesting experience for me
